## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)
Global strategic developments are dominated by escalating tensions between the United States and Iran, following renewed strikes and the revocation of Iran's oil export waiver by the U.S., further straining a fragile interim agreement. NATO leaders convened in Ankara, Türkiye, to discuss collective security and defense spending, with the summit also revealing strains within the alliance amidst President Trump's strong rhetoric regarding European allies' contributions and his claims on Greenland. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has modestly downgraded its global economic growth forecast for 2026 to 3%, citing the energy shock from the Iran conflict, though partially offset by booming investment in artificial intelligence and other technologies. Global capital markets experienced turbulence, with stock futures falling and oil prices surging due to the heightened geopolitical risks in the Middle East. Canada announced the backing of nine nations for a new Canada-led Defence, Security and Resilience Bank, aimed at mobilizing private capital for defense initiatives.

---

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)
### Americas
In Colombia, President-elect Abelardo de la Espriella has suspended the transition process with outgoing President Gustavo Petro after allegations of fraud in the June runoff election. Leftist candidate Iván Cepeda also raised concerns of fraud, accusing Espriella of working for the Trump administration. Canada announced the support of eight other nations for a new Defence, Security and Resilience Bank, headquartered in Canada, to provide low-cost financing for defense projects, with the goal of being operational by 2027. The 2026 FIFA World Cup is being leveraged for diplomatic ends, fostering international cooperation and development.

### Europe
Ukraine launched strikes against multiple Russian oil refineries, tankers, the Borisoglebsk airfield, and road bridges, causing explosions and fires, and resulting in at least two casualties. In France, an appeals court upheld Marine Le Pen's conviction for misuse of European Parliament funds but allowed her to run for president in 2027, despite being sentenced to an electronic monitor. Nigel Farage has resigned his Clacton parliamentary seat, triggering a by-election amidst scrutiny over his undeclared finances, which major parties have refused to contest, framing it as a "people versus the establishment" battle. The European Union's General Court upheld Apple's designation under the Digital Markets Act, compelling the company to open parts of its ecosystem to rivals. The European Commission also adopted its July infringement procedures and issued €11 billion in its seventh syndicated transaction of 2026. Denmark's Prime Minister reiterated readiness to defend NATO territory following remarks from President Trump concerning Greenland and potential U.S. troop withdrawal from Europe.

### Asia-Pacific
China launched a submarine-launched ballistic missile (SLBM) into international waters in the Pacific Ocean, an action Japan and the U.S. deem a threat to regional security. Japan's Foreign Minister Motegi Toshimitsu held meetings with his German and U.S. and ROK counterparts on the margins of the NATO Summit, reaffirming cooperation in security, defense, and promoting a "Free and Open Indo-Pacific". Malaysian ports have maintained stability and recorded higher shipping volumes despite Middle East tensions, as vessels reroute. In Pakistan, security forces engaged militants across Balochistan province over the past four days, resulting in 54 terrorists and 42 others, including security personnel and civilians, being killed. Sri Lanka's foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows increased to $1.04 billion in 2025 from $759 million in 2024. Myanmar's general public continues to overwhelmingly reject military rule, according to sentiment analysis of Facebook reactions.

### Africa
Ghana postponed a planned August visit by South African President Cyril Ramaphosa due to rising diplomatic tensions over anti-foreigner protests in South Africa and conflicting accounts of deaths. Uganda has launched a nationwide enforcement campaign against illegal public Wi-Fi service providers to protect consumers and safeguard critical communications infrastructure. The Lobito Corridor railway in Angola, a key export route for critical minerals, reached financial close on a $753 million financing package for rehabilitation. The World Health Organization (WHO) has warned that the Bundibugyo strain of the Ebola virus disease continues to expand in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), with over 1,561 confirmed cases and 506 deaths. France delivered 20 tons of aid to the DRC to assist in containing the Ebola epidemic. Uganda has also confirmed a Marburg virus case in a toddler during Ebola surveillance efforts.

## SECTION 3 — Gulf & MENA Region
The Gulf and MENA region is experiencing significant geopolitical and economic shifts, marked by heightened tensions between the U.S. and Iran following fresh U.S. strikes and the revocation of Iran's oil export waiver. Qatar and Kuwait have condemned Iranian attacks on their soil and commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, calling for diplomacy to de-escalate tensions. Economically, the MENA region emerged as one of the fastest-growing investment destinations in 2025, with foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows rising by 21% to US$111 billion, driven by economic diversification, industrial localization, and digital transformation. Saudi Arabia, in particular, is attracting substantial FDI as it transforms into a diversified industrial and technology economy under its Vision 2030 reforms.

## SECTION 4 — Technology and the Future
Artificial Intelligence (AI) continues to be a central focus, with booming investment globally. DeepSeek is developing an AI chip, indicating a strategic shift in technology. The World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) reported that investment in intangible assets, including software, data, brands, and other intellectual property, surpassed US$10 trillion in 2025, growing over three times faster than tangible investment. AI is transforming robotic operations in space exploration, enabling spacecraft to navigate hazardous terrains and conduct scientific missions autonomously, overcoming communication delays. Major technology conferences, such as LEAP East Hong Kong and the World CIO 200 Summit in Riyadh, are addressing AI, digital transformation, and smart city development. Gemini 3.5 Pro, an anticipated frontier model, faces delays in its general availability, with Google citing token efficiency and coding performance issues.

## SECTION 5 — Tech Industry & Global Business Shifts
Apple faced a setback in Europe as the EU General Court upheld its "gatekeeper" designation under the Digital Markets Act, requiring the company to open its ecosystem to rivals. The ruling preserves the EU's efforts to curb the power of large digital platforms, extending obligations to companies like Alphabet, Amazon, ByteDance, Meta, and Microsoft. Faraday Future is advancing its Middle East EAI robotics strategy through cooperation frameworks and regional ecosystem collaboration across the UAE and GCC, focusing on AI, smart cities, and digital transformation. India's fitness chain Cult.fit has applied for an Initial Public Offering (IPO) to raise approximately Rs 4,000 crore. Reliance's Strand Life Sciences has secured an Indian patent for its cancer detection technology. (Critical Data Injection).

AI is profoundly influencing robotic space exploration, with innovations enabling autonomous navigation and high-value scientific data collection on missions to Mars and beyond. This advancement is crucial for overcoming significant communication delays between Earth and deep space missions. Discussions at events like Confluence's annual Thought Series highlight the increasing convergence of AI, space exploration, and sustainability in shaping future scientific and technological landscapes.

## SECTION 7 — Global Health & Biosecurity Watch
The World Health Organization (WHO) has issued warnings regarding the expanding Bundibugyo strain of the Ebola virus disease in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), with confirmed cases exceeding 1,561 and 506 deaths. Response efforts are challenged by conflict, displacement, and limited resources, with treatment centers overwhelmed. France has delivered 20 tons of aid, including infection control equipment and medicine, to the DRC to support local healthcare efforts. Uganda has also reported a confirmed Marburg virus case in a toddler in the Kyegegwa District, identified during Ebola surveillance, with contacts currently being monitored and showing no symptoms.

The U.S. launched strikes against 80 Iranian targets in response to recent attacks on commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's central military command has warned of "decisive actions," claiming its combat drones struck U.S. positions in Bahrain and Kuwait, and stating that "all U.S. bases in the region will be legitimate targets". Ukraine conducted drone attacks on Russian oil refineries, petrochemical plants, tankers, and an airfield, resulting in casualties and significant damage, as part of its campaign to disrupt Russian fuel supplies. In Pakistan, military operations against militants in Balochistan resulted in the deaths of 54 terrorists and 42 others, including security forces and civilians, over four days. The National Investigation Agency (NIA) conducted coordinated searches at 20 locations across 10 Indian states, including Telangana and Delhi, in connection with an online terror radicalisation network linked to ISIS and Al-Qaeda in the Indian Subcontinent (AQIS). ## SECTION 9 — Energy, Climate & Environment Watch
Crude oil prices surged following renewed U.S.-Iran tensions, with Brent crude climbing over 7% to approximately US$80 per barrel and WTI near US$74 per barrel. This escalation has revived concerns about disruptions to global energy supplies through the Strait of Hormuz and potential inflationary pressures. The IMF's updated forecast assumes the Strait of Hormuz will gradually reopen from mid-July, reaching pre-war conditions by March 2027. Earlier forecasts indicated that intensifying El Niño conditions were likely to drive energy and food insecurity globally in the coming months, with direct operational impacts on maritime operations due to lower water levels. India is planning to establish its first strategic Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) reserves to bolster energy security. (Critical Data Injection).

## SECTION 10 — Global Economy & Market Signals
**Crude Oil:** Prices surged by 7-8% on July 8, with Brent crude reaching approximately $80/Bbl and WTI near $74/Bbl, driven by escalating US-Iran tensions and concerns over Strait of Hormuz disruptions.
**Equities:** Global equity markets experienced broad-based selling, with the S&P 500 falling 1% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average dropping 831 points (1.6%). Asian indices, including Japan's Nikkei 225 and South Korea's Kospi, traded lower, with the Kospi entering bear-market territory due to a sell-off in semiconductor stocks. Major tech stocks, particularly the "magnificent seven," recorded losses.
**Currency:** The US Dollar Index (DXY) rose slightly to around 101.2, up 0.17% from the previous session, strengthened by safe-haven demand amidst Middle East tensions and expectations of potential Federal Reserve rate hikes. The Japanese Yen remained near a 40-year low, while the Euro and British Pound edged lower against the dollar.
**Central Banks:** The U.S. Federal Reserve released the minutes of its June meeting, which showed the committee kept interest rates unchanged at 3.50%-3.75%, but the overall tone was hawkish, with nine out of 19 policymakers expecting at least one more rate hike before the end of 2026. This is fueling expectations of further monetary tightening.
**Inflation Indicators:** The IMF raised its 2026 headline inflation forecast by 0.3 percentage points to 4.7% from April, expecting energy prices to remain 25% higher than before the war. Concerns over inflation are exacerbated by surging oil prices and potential supply disruptions.

The accused in an 11-year-old's rape-murder case in Baruipur, West Bengal, was killed in a police encounter. ## SECTION 14 — Telangana State Updates
Telangana will host the Khelo India Youth Games 2026 in November, as announced by Union Sports Minister Dr. Mansukh Mandaviya. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast strong surface winds, reaching 50 to 60 kmph, in 12 Telangana districts for today. The Telangana Movement Activists Identification Committee commenced its identification drive, starting with consultations with university representatives. US insurance and financial services giant Nationwide inaugurated its Global Services Center in Hyderabad to bolster its worldwide operations and foster innovation. Telangana Chief Minister A Revanth Reddy launched a free breakfast and lunch program for students from Nursery to Class 12 to enhance nutrition. Telangana Deputy Chief Minister Mallu Bhatti Vikramarka accused the Centre of misrepresenting the Tadicherla-II coal block situation, clarifying it was a delayed mining lease approval rather than a new allocation. The fifth Telangana State Police Sports Meet-2026 was inaugurated in Hyderabad, featuring a new five-cluster format designed to encourage broader participation among police personnel. BRS working president KT Rama Rao issued a legal notice to Deccan Chronicle, alleging defamation over a report connecting him to building permissions near Hussain Sagar. Booth Level Officers (BLOs) in Hyderabad and other districts are encountering difficulties in tracing voters for enumeration forms due to irregular house numbering. Telangana has launched a MeeSeva facility for booking urea.

## SECTION 17 — Social Media & Public Sentiment Trends
A significant youth-led protest movement, dubbed the "Cockroach Janta Party" (CJP), has emerged in India, sparked by a satirical social media post after a chief justice's controversial remarks. This movement, driven primarily by Gen Z students, has transitioned from online virality to street protests, demanding the resignation of the Education Minister over alleged systemic failures. In the U.S., concerns are growing over government actions perceived as cracking down on dissent, with instances of federal agents reportedly investigating individuals for online criticism of the administration, raising First Amendment concerns. Globally, social media regulation for children is a growing debate, with some countries implementing bans for minors, while the U.S. sees a push for stricter online safety legislation following landmark jury verdicts against tech companies. The Reuters Institute Digital News Report 2026 highlights a turbulent news environment, with social media and video creators gaining popularity as news sources amidst falling trust in traditional media. It also notes a rise in AI chatbot usage for news, particularly among younger demographics.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News
**What Happened:** On July 8, 2026, the United States launched strikes against over 80 Iranian targets and revoked a waiver allowing Iranian oil sales, following a series of Iranian attacks on commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. President Donald Trump declared the interim ceasefire agreement "over" and threatened further action, while Iran warned of "decisive actions" and claimed its drones struck U.S. bases in Bahrain and Kuwait.
**Root Causes:** The immediate cause is Iran's alleged attacks on commercial vessels in the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz, which the U.S. views as a violation of the fragile interim agreement signed on June 17. Underlying factors include long-standing geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and Iran, regional power struggles, and control over crucial oil shipping lanes.
**Historical Background:** Tensions in the Strait of Hormuz have been a recurring flashpoint, with previous incidents involving attacks on tankers and threats to maritime navigation. The current escalation follows an interim agreement, which both sides now accuse the other of violating. The U.S. has a history of imposing sanctions and military responses to perceived Iranian provocations, while Iran has consistently vowed to respond to actions it considers aggressive.
**Key Actors:** Key actors include the United States (President Donald Trump, U.S. Central Command, Treasury Department), Iran (military command, foreign ministry), and regional allies and partners such as Qatar and Kuwait, who have condemned the escalation. NATO allies have also been drawn into discussions on the implications for collective security.
**Strategic Importance:** The Strait of Hormuz is a critical chokepoint through which a significant portion of the world's crude oil and natural gas passes. Any disruption here has immediate and profound global economic consequences, as evidenced by the surge in oil prices. The escalation also tests alliances, global diplomacy, and the potential for a wider regional conflict.
**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** Long-term consequences could include sustained higher energy prices, increased global inflation, and further volatility in financial markets. It risks derailing ongoing peace negotiations and could lead to a prolonged period of instability in the Middle East. There could also be shifts in global trade routes as countries seek alternatives to the Strait of Hormuz, impacting shipping and logistics chains.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)
*   **1497:** Vasco da Gama embarked from Lisbon on his pioneering voyage to India, establishing the first direct sea route from Europe to Asia.
*   **1777:** Vermont adopted its constitution, becoming the first region in what would become the United States to abolish slavery.
*   **1889:** The first issue of The Wall Street Journal was published, becoming a globally influential financial newspaper.
*   **1932:** The Dow Jones Industrial Average reached its lowest point during the Great Depression, closing at 41.22.
*   **1937:** Turkey, Iran, Iraq, and Afghanistan signed the Treaty of Saadabad, a non-aggression pact.
*   **1947:** Reports of a UFO crash-landing in Roswell, New Mexico, were broadcast, initiating the famous Roswell UFO incident.
*   **1948:** The United States Air Force accepted its first female recruits into the Women's Air Force (WAF) program.
*   **1994:** Kim Il Sung, North Korea's communist leader, died, and his son Kim Jong Il began to assume supreme leadership.
*   **2011:** Space Shuttle Atlantis was launched on its final mission, marking the end of the U.S. Space Shuttle program.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)
*   **1497:** Vasco da Gama set sail from Lisbon, embarking on the historic voyage that would establish the first direct sea route between Europe and India.
*   **1858:** Lord Canning officially declared peace in India after the fall of Gwalior Fort, marking the end of the Indian Mutiny of 1857. This led to the Government of India Act of 1858, abolishing East India Company rule in favor of direct British government rule.
*   **1918:** The Montagu-Chelmsford Report, which laid the groundwork for the Government of India Act 1919 and introduced a bicameral legislature, was published.
*   **1925:** Dr. M. S. Swaminathan, the architect of India's Green Revolution, whose work transformed India into a leading food producer, was born.
*   **1958:** Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru inaugurated the Bhakra-Nangal hydroelectric project on the world's largest canal in Punjab.
*   **1972:** Sourav Ganguly, a prominent Indian cricketer and former national team captain, was born.
*   **2023:** U.S. authorities charged Indian gangster Lawrence Bishnoi and his associate Goldy Brar with orchestrating the assassination of Khalistani separatist Hardeep Singh Nijjar in Canada in November 2023. (Critical Data Injection).

## SECTION 21 — This Week in Review (Last 7 Days)
The week has been dominated by a significant escalation of tensions between the U.S. and Iran, marked by U.S. strikes and the revocation of Iran's oil export waiver following attacks on commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, driving global oil prices higher. The NATO Summit in Ankara highlighted alliance strains, particularly regarding defense spending and U.S. President Trump's demands. Economically, the IMF downgraded its global growth forecast for 2026, citing the Middle East conflict, while central banks, including the U.S. Federal Reserve, maintained a hawkish stance on interest rates amidst inflation concerns. Ukraine continued its strategic strikes against Russian oil infrastructure, causing significant damage. Political developments in Europe included Marine Le Pen's presidential bid despite a conviction and Nigel Farage's by-election in the UK. In Asia, China's SLBM launch raised regional security concerns. African nations grappled with an expanding Ebola outbreak in the DRC and migration tensions between Ghana and South Africa.

---

## SECTION 22 — This Month in Review (From 1st of Current Month)
The early part of July has been defined by the sharp escalation of the U.S.-Iran conflict, leading to U.S. military strikes and increased oil market volatility as the Strait of Hormuz remains a critical flashpoint. The NATO Summit served as a key diplomatic event, addressing alliance cohesion, defense commitments, and support for Ukraine. The global economic outlook was tempered by the IMF's revised growth forecast due to the Middle East conflict, alongside persistent inflation concerns influencing central bank policies, particularly the U.S. Federal Reserve's hawkish posture. Ukraine has continued its campaign of drone attacks on Russian energy infrastructure, impacting fuel supplies and regional stability. European politics saw notable developments with Marine Le Pen's presidential campaign launch and Nigel Farage triggering a by-election amidst financial scrutiny. In the Asia-Pacific, China's SLBM launch underscored regional security challenges. Health crises, notably the expanding Ebola outbreak in the DRC, and regional migration tensions in Africa have also been prominent.

## SECTION 25 — Strategic Insight / Underreported Development
An underreported development is the accelerating shift of global economic value from physical to intangible assets, with investment in software, data, brands, and other intellectual property-backed assets surpassing US$10 trillion in 2025. This trend, highlighted by the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO), indicates a fundamental structural change in investment composition, growing more than three times faster than tangible investment. The U.S. accounts for nearly half of this intangible investment, suggesting a widening gap in innovation-driven economic leadership. This shift has profound implications for national competitiveness, labor markets, and the future of global supply chains, favoring economies with advanced digital infrastructure and innovation ecosystems.
